confidence.disclosure: Nur al-Din Abd al-Rahman ibn Ahmad Jami, the last great classical Persian poet, is well documented in Timurid sources, especially in the works of his close friend the polymath Mir Ali Shir Nawa'i and in the chronicles Habib al-Siyar (Khwandamir) and Rauzat al-Safa (Mirkhwand). His birth in Jam, his education in Herat, his enrolment in the Naqshbandi Sufi order under Sa'd al-Din Kashghari, his many works, his journey to Baghdad and Mecca in 1472, his patronage by the Aq Qoyunlu and the Timurids, and his death in Herat in 1492 are firmly placed by year and several by day.

sources: Mir Ali Shir Nawa'i, Khamsat al-Mutahayyirin

journey.event: Born on 23 Sha'ban 817 AH (7 November 1414) in the village of Kharjird in the district of Jam in Khurasan (modern eastern Iran), from which he takes his pen name Jami. His family is of religious learning, with a long line of jurists and scholars on his father's side.

When he is about five years old, his father moves the family to Herat, the great Timurid capital, then at the height of its cultural splendour under the rule of Shah Rukh and his cultivated wife Gawhar Shad. Jami spends almost his entire life there.

Studies at the leading madrasas of Herat, mastering the religious sciences (tafsir, hadith, fiqh, kalam), the rational sciences (logic, mathematics, astronomy), and the literary arts. His teachers describe him as the most gifted student they have ever taught.

Around 1436 travels to Samarkand to continue his studies at the famous madrasa of Ulugh Beg. Studies mathematics and astronomy with the master scholars there, including Qadi Zada al-Rumi. Already so accomplished that even the senior scholars of Samarkand are said to defer to him in disputation.

Returns to Herat. Around this time undergoes a spiritual crisis and is drawn to Sufism. Joins the Naqshbandi order under the master Sa'd al-Din Kashghari, who has settled in Herat. Devotes himself to the inner path and receives intensive training over a number of years.

On the death of Sa'd al-Din Kashghari around 1456, Jami succeeds (informally) to the leadership of the Naqshbandi presence in Herat under the spiritual authority of Khwaja Ubayd Allah Ahrar in Samarkand. Becomes one of the most respected Sufi voices of his age.

Becomes the close friend and spiritual adviser of the brilliant Mir Ali Shir Nawa'i, the great patron of arts and chief minister of the new Timurid sultan Sultan Husayn Bayqara. Through Nawa'i's patronage Jami's later works are produced in beautiful illuminated manuscripts at the Herat workshops, the most famous in the Islamic world.

In 1472 leaves Herat to make the pilgrimage to Mecca. Travels west via Nishapur, Mashhad, Hamadan, and Tabriz. At Tabriz is honoured by the Aq Qoyunlu sultan Uzun Hasan, who tries unsuccessfully to recruit him to his court.

Continues to Baghdad where he is received with great honour by the local elites and visits the tombs of the early Sufi masters - al-Junayd, al-Sari al-Saqati, al-Hallaj. Also visits Karbala and Najaf.

Performs the hajj to Mecca in 1473, then visits Medina and the tomb of the Prophet. Returns to Khurasan via Damascus, where he meets the leading scholars and is treated with great honour by the Mamluk authorities. The journey, his only major travel from Herat, is itself a kind of public manifestation of his spiritual prestige.

Composes Yusuf and Zulaykha, his mathnawi version of the Quranic story of Joseph and Potiphar's wife, dedicated to his patron Sultan Husayn Bayqara. It becomes the most famous version of the story in any language and one of the most popular Persian poems of the early modern period.

Composes (around 1485-1487) the Nafahat al-Uns, his Persian version of al-Sulami's Tabaqat al-Sufiyya, a collection of biographies of more than 600 Sufi masters. The Nafahat al-Uns becomes the standard reference work on Sufi hagiography in the Persianate world.

Dies in Herat on 18 Muharram 898 AH (9 November 1492), aged 78. The funeral is led by Sultan Husayn Bayqara himself, with Mir Ali Shir Nawa'i, princes, and the leading scholars in attendance. He is buried in Herat near the tomb of his Sufi master Sa'd al-Din Kashghari. His grave is still a place of visitation. He is considered the seal of classical Persian poetry - after him, the great Persian poetic tradition turns to new directions in Mughal India and Safavid Iran.
